author: @CPedersen2012
text:
First, I have really enjoyed many of your videos.  Thank you for you your work - and you publishing your work.  You have been the first channel I have subscribed to.

I have seen where you make graphene, as well as ink.

Have you thought about the process of combining those two processes into one whereby the goal is a single step production of graphene ink.

I ask for a reason other than laziness.  Inkjet printers require inks with known stabilities and viscosities.  One of the major issues with inkjet is particle sizes for clogging.

Graphene particle size  can probably be controlled by the speed of precipitation (guessing?) or by the method of formation and hence my question have you bent your brain to the question of graphene ink production?

Amazed....

Chris

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have indeed - i just have no use for it right now

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i know that might seem like an odd reply - but graphene as a single layer is not as useful for the home experimenter as you might think. Graphene is actually quite difficult to use well and needs a whole host of pretty expensive equipment to actually do anything with it - my channel is about inspiring the home inventor - so i haven't done that much in using monolayers - but i do have something up and coming

replies.author: @CPedersen2012
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith
Hey Mr. Murray-Smith

I look forward to hearing about it!

I'm a home experimenter and I've used silver nanoparticles to print circuits using inkjet printers.  The issues of annealing, stability and adsorption were difficult.

Pardon enthusiasm -  but the possibilities of printing circuits is something that is cool and exciting!  

Another question:
If you took one of your graphene electrodes and hooked it up to a capacitor say from a pocket camera or a strobe light.. wouldn't you vaporize portions of the graphene and cause graphene deposition?  Might also work with graphite, no?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Chris Pedersen
i don't know i have never tried it - but yes it probably would - having said that you get graphene in a candle flame - just not much of it and it is difficult to extract

author: @Gunnahan
text:
In my 20s I did a lot of airbrushing (being a graphic artist) and I think the drop in Air-pressure on long bursts is due to the compressors capacity. There are quite affordable compressors on the market that will supply ample pressure with a big tank without having to switch on the motor. That might help.
great demonstration there, sir. I think that will open a whole new world of possible applications for your ink :)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: cheers mate - the airbrush i got was pretty cheap but it was only for the demo so i don't mind but good suggestion thanks

author: @Candyman97
text:
Hey Robert,
  here is an idea for you. Since you have proved, you can use it in an air brush. Then the next step would be too get an empty ink jet cartridge, fill it with your ink, then use it in an ink jet printer! Now you could print different designs, and see how they interact! Yes I already know I'm evil, and somewhat mad, and you're a scientist. Together, we could be an evil mad scientist, and take over the would!(Evil laugh, for dramatic effect!) but I digress. Still it would be interesting, if you could get it to work!(Again evil laugh for dramatic effect!) lol

author: @gr1f1th
text:
I was going to try this but I seem to have misplaced my airbrush. Glad to know it will work once I find it. :D
btw tried the Thremocell the other day with copper tape->ink->zinc oxide->aluminium and got .5v until it dried out.... then I got nothing. Do I need to press the cell or do you think it's shorting out? Somewhat lost as to what to try next.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: when this kind of thing happens to me i just do it again but more carefully lol

replies.author: @gr1f1th
replies.text: Robert Murray-Smith Yeah I'm liking the method of painting the zinc oxide paint on the aluminium and the ink on the copper tape and pressing them together after letting them dry a bit. Otherwise the ink mixes with the zinc oxide when applying the second coat and I just get a mess. I think I'll try again but tape off the edges better. Been messing with my 3D printer and it's divided my attention..... not to mention I got another kid on the way in 4 weeks. Not enough hours in the day man! *sigh*

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
ben dorman
sorry mate - but i know exactly what you mean!
